sentences.chinese: 李廣，隴西成紀人也。
sentences.literal: Li Guang was a man of Chengji in Longxi.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang came from Chengji in Longxi commandery.

sentences.chinese: 其先曰李信，秦時為將，逐得燕太子丹者也。
sentences.literal: His ancestor was called Li Xin; in Qin times he was a general—the one who pursued and captured Crown Prince Dan of Yan.
sentences.idiomatic: His forebear Li Xin had served as a Qin general and was the officer who ran down and captured the crown prince of Yan, Dan.

sentences.chinese: 廣世世受射。
sentences.literal: For generations the Guang family received instruction in archery.
sentences.idiomatic: Archery had been handed down in Li Guang's family for generations.

sentences.chinese: 孝文十四年，匈奴大入蕭關，而廣以良家子從軍擊胡，用善射，殺首虜多，為郎，騎常侍。
sentences.literal: In the fourteenth year of Emperor Xiaowen's reign the Xiongnu entered in force at Xiaoguan, and Guang, as a son of a respectable household, joined the army to strike the Hu; because he was skilled at archery and killed many enemy heads and captives, he was made a gentleman-attendant and cavalry attendant-in-ordinary.
sentences.idiomatic: In 166 BCE the Xiongnu poured through Xiaoguan. Li Guang enlisted as a young man of good family, fought them with the army, and distinguished himself with the bow—so many kills that he was appointed gentleman-attendant and then cavalry attendant-in-ordinary at court.

sentences.chinese: 數從射獵，格殺猛獸，文帝曰：「惜廣不逢時，令當高祖世，萬戶侯豈足道哉！」
sentences.literal: Repeatedly he accompanied [the emperor] on hunts and shoots, grappling with and killing fierce beasts. Emperor Wen said, "It is a pity Guang does not meet his season; had he lived in the age of Gaozu, a marquisate of ten thousand households—how could that be worth mentioning!"
sentences.idiomatic: He often rode out on the hunt with the emperor and killed dangerous game at close quarters. Emperor Wen remarked, "What a pity Li Guang was not born in Gaozu's day—a mere marquisate of ten thousand households would have been beneath him."

sentences.chinese: 匈奴侵上郡，上使中貴人從廣勒習兵擊匈奴。
sentences.literal: The Xiongnu invaded Shang commandery; the emperor sent a palace favorite to follow Guang in drilling troops to strike the Xiongnu.
sentences.idiomatic: When the Xiongnu raided Shang commandery, the emperor dispatched a eunuch favorite to train under Li Guang and practice campaigning against them.

sentences.chinese: 中貴人者數十騎從，見匈奴三人，與戰。
sentences.literal: The palace favorite had several dozen horsemen following him; they saw three Xiongnu and fought them.
sentences.idiomatic: The favorite rode out with a few dozen cavalry, encountered three Xiongnu horsemen, and engaged them.

sentences.chinese: 射傷中貴人，殺其騎且盡。
sentences.literal: They shot and wounded the palace favorite and killed his riders nearly to the last man.
sentences.idiomatic: The Xiongnu wounded the favorite with their bows and all but wiped out his escort.

sentences.chinese: 中貴人走廣，廣曰：「是必射鵰者也。」
sentences.literal: The palace favorite fled to Guang; Guang said, "These must be men who shoot the great eagle."
sentences.idiomatic: The favorite galloped to Li Guang, who said, "Those were Xiongnu eagle-archers—men who can bring down a golden eagle on the wing."

sentences.chinese: 廣乃從百騎往馳三人。
sentences.literal: Guang then took a hundred riders and galloped after the three men.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang took a hundred horsemen and rode hard after the three.

sentences.chinese: 三人亡馬步行，行數十里。
sentences.literal: The three had lost their horses and walked on foot for several tens of li.
sentences.idiomatic: They had lost their mounts and were walking—already many li from where they had fought.

sentences.chinese: 廣令其騎張左右翼，而廣身自射彼三人者，殺其二人，生得一人，果匈奴射鵰者也。
sentences.literal: Guang ordered his riders to spread the left and right wings, while Guang himself shot at those three men, killed two of them, and captured one alive; they proved indeed to be Xiongnu eagle-archers.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang fanned his men to left and right, then shot the three himself—two dead, one taken alive—and they turned out to be the eagle-archers after all.

sentences.chinese: 已縛之上山，望匈奴數千騎，見廣，以為誘騎，驚，上山陳。
sentences.literal: Having bound him, they went up a hill; looking out they saw several thousand Xiongnu riders. When they saw Guang, they took them for bait cavalry, were alarmed, and went up the hill to form battle array.
sentences.idiomatic: They had tied up their prisoner and climbed a rise when they sighted thousands of enemy horse. The Xiongnu mistook Li Guang's tiny band for a decoy, hesitated, and drew up on the heights in formation.

sentences.chinese: 廣之百騎皆大恐，欲馳還走。
sentences.literal: Guang's hundred riders were all greatly frightened and wished to gallop back in flight.
sentences.idiomatic: His hundred men panicked and wanted to wheel and run for it.

sentences.chinese: 廣曰：「我去大軍數十里，今如此走，匈奴追射，我立盡。
sentences.literal: Guang said, "We are several tens of li from the main army; if we flee like this now, the Xiongnu will pursue and shoot us—we will be finished at once.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang said, "We are dozens of li from the main force. If we bolt now, they'll run us down and pick us off—we'll be dead in moments.

sentences.chinese: 今我留，匈奴必以我為大軍之誘，不我擊。」
sentences.literal: If we now remain, the Xiongnu are sure to take us for bait for a large army and will not strike us."
sentences.idiomatic: If we stay put, they'll assume we're bait for a larger ambush and hold their fire."

sentences.chinese: 廣令曰：「前！」
sentences.literal: Guang ordered, "Forward!"
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang gave the order: "Advance!"

sentences.chinese: 未到匈奴陳二里所，止，令曰：「皆下馬解鞍！」
sentences.literal: When they had not yet reached within about two li of the Xiongnu formation, he halted and ordered, "All dismount and unstrap the saddles!"
sentences.idiomatic: They rode to within about two li of the enemy line, then he halted the column and shouted, "Dismount—loosen every saddle girth!"

sentences.chinese: 騎曰：「虜多如是，解鞍，即急，奈何？」
sentences.literal: The riders said, "The enemy are so many; if we unstrap the saddles, when it becomes urgent, what then?"
sentences.idiomatic: His men protested: "There are so many of them—if we take off our saddles and things turn ugly, what then?"

sentences.chinese: 廣曰：「彼虜以我為走，今解鞍以示不去，用堅其意。」
sentences.literal: Guang said, "Those barbarians take us for men who will flee; now by unstrapping the saddles we show we are not going away, thereby to make their judgment firm."
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang replied, "They think we're running. Dismounting and stripping the gear shows we mean to stay—that will fix their idea that we're only a screen for something bigger."

sentences.chinese: 有白馬將出護兵。
sentences.literal: There was a white-horse general who came out to oversee the troops.
sentences.idiomatic: A Xiongnu officer on a white horse rode out to supervise his men.

sentences.chinese: 廣上馬，與十餘騎奔射殺白馬將，而復還至其百騎中，解鞍，縱馬臥。
sentences.literal: Guang mounted his horse; with more than ten riders he galloped and shot, killing the white-horse general, then returned again to his hundred riders, unstrapped the saddles, and let the horses lie down.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang vaulted into the saddle, took a dozen riders in a dash, killed the white-horse commander with a volley, then cantered back to his line, loosened the saddles again, and let the horses graze and lie down as before.

sentences.chinese: 時會暮，胡兵終怪之，弗敢擊。
sentences.literal: It happened to be dusk; the Hu troops in the end found it strange and did not dare attack.
sentences.idiomatic: Dusk was falling; the enemy remained baffled by the display and still would not close.

sentences.chinese: 夜半，胡兵以為漢有伏軍於傍欲夜取之，即引去。
sentences.literal: In the middle of the night the Hu troops thought the Han had ambush troops at the side intending to take them by night, and so withdrew.
sentences.idiomatic: Around midnight they decided Han ambushers must be waiting to strike in the dark, and broke camp.

sentences.chinese: 平旦，廣乃歸其大軍。
sentences.literal: At daybreak Guang then returned to his main army.
sentences.idiomatic: At first light Li Guang rejoined the main column.

sentences.chinese: 後徙為隴西、北地、雁門中雲中太守。
sentences.literal: Afterward he was transferred to be governor of Longxi, Beidi, Yanmen, Dai, and Yunzhong.
sentences.idiomatic: He was later posted in turn as governor of Longxi, Beidi, Yanmen, Dai commandery, and Yunzhong along the northern frontier.

sentences.chinese: 後漢誘單于以馬邑城，使大軍伏馬邑傍，而廣為驍騎將軍，屬護軍將軍。
sentences.literal: Afterward the Han lured the Chanyu with Mayi city, had a great army lie in ambush beside Mayi, and Guang was made dashing-cavalry general, subordinate to the protector-general.
sentences.idiomatic: Later the court tried to lure the Chanyu into Mayi with a ruse while the main army lay in wait; Li Guang was appointed dashing-cavalry general under the protector-general's command.

sentences.chinese: 單于覺之，去，漢軍皆無功。
sentences.literal: The Chanyu perceived it and departed; the Han armies all achieved no merit.
sentences.idiomatic: The Chanyu saw through the trap and withdrew, and the Han expedition came away with nothing.

sentences.chinese: 後四歲，廣以衛尉為將軍，出雁門擊匈奴。
sentences.literal: Four years later Guang, as commandant of the guards, became a general and went out through Yanmen to strike the Xiongnu.
sentences.idiomatic: Four years after that he left the capital as commandant of the guards, took the title of general, and marched out through Yanmen against the Xiongnu.

sentences.chinese: 匈奴兵多，破廣軍，生得廣。
sentences.literal: The Xiongnu troops were many; they defeated Guang's army and captured Guang alive.
sentences.idiomatic: The enemy outnumbered him, broke his force, and took Li Guang prisoner.

sentences.chinese: 單于素聞廣賢，令曰：「得李廣必生致之。」
sentences.literal: The Chanyu had long heard that Guang was worthy and ordered, "If Li Guang is taken, he must be brought in alive."
sentences.idiomatic: The Chanyu had heard of Li Guang's reputation and issued orders: "Li Guang is to be captured alive."

sentences.chinese: 胡騎得廣，廣時傷，置兩馬間。
sentences.literal: The Hu riders got Guang; Guang at the time was wounded; they placed him between two horses.
sentences.idiomatic: The horsemen had him—wounded—and slung him in a rope cradle between two mounts.

sentences.chinese: 絡而盛臥。
sentences.literal: They netted him and packed him lying down.
sentences.idiomatic: They bound him in a net and carried him along supine between the animals.

sentences.chinese: 行十餘里，廣陽死，睨其傍有一兒騎善馬，暫騰而上胡兒馬，因抱兒鞭馬南馳數十里，得其餘軍。
sentences.literal: Having gone more than ten li, Guang feigned death; he squinted and saw beside him a youth riding a fine horse; in a moment he leaped up onto the Hu youth's horse, then embracing the youth he whipped the horse and galloped south several tens of li, reaching the rest of his army.
sentences.idiomatic: After some ten li he feigned death, spotted a young rider on a fine horse at his side, vaulted onto the animal behind him, locked an arm around the boy to hold him fast, and spurred south for dozens of li until he reached the rest of his troops.

sentences.chinese: 匈奴騎數百追之，廣行取兒弓射殺追騎，以故得脫。
sentences.literal: Several hundred Xiongnu riders pursued him; Guang along the way took the youth's bow and shot and killed the pursuing riders; by this means he got away.
sentences.idiomatic: Hundreds of pursuers closed on him; he snatched the lad's bow as he rode and picked off the leaders until they broke off the chase.

sentences.chinese: 於是至漢，漢下廣吏。
sentences.literal: Thereupon he reached the Han; the Han handed Guang over to the officials.
sentences.idiomatic: Back on Han soil the court turned him over to the judiciary for trial.

sentences.chinese: 吏當廣亡失多，為虜所生得，當斬，贖為庶人。
sentences.literal: The officials judged Guang for many losses and flight and for being taken alive by the enemy—liable to execution; he ransomed himself and became a commoner.
sentences.idiomatic: The judges found him guilty of heavy casualties and capture by the enemy—capital crimes—but he commuted the sentence with a fine and was stripped of rank.

sentences.chinese: 數歲，與故穎陰侯屏居藍田南山中射獵。
sentences.literal: For several years, together with the former Marquis of Yingyin he lived in retirement in the southern hills of Lantian, hunting with bow.
sentences.idiomatic: For some years he lived in seclusion in the Lantian hills with the former Marquis of Yingyin, passing the days hunting.

sentences.chinese: 嘗夜從一騎出，從人田間飲。
sentences.literal: Once at night he went out with one rider following and drank in someone's field.
sentences.idiomatic: One night he rode out with a single attendant and stopped at a farmhouse for wine.

sentences.chinese: 還至亭，霸陵尉醉，呵止廣，廣騎曰：「故李將軍。」
sentences.literal: Returning they reached a post station; the Baling post captain was drunk and shouted to stop Guang; Guang's rider said, "The former General Li."
sentences.idiomatic: On the way back they were halted at a courier station by a drunken captain of Baling, who challenged them; Li Guang's man called out, "That is the former General Li."

sentences.chinese: 尉曰：「今將軍尚不得夜行，何故也！」
sentences.literal: The captain said, "The present general still may not travel by night—how much more so in your case!"
sentences.idiomatic: The captain retorted, "Even an active general is not allowed through here after dark—what business have you?"

sentences.chinese: 宿廣亭下。
sentences.literal: He lodged Guang beneath the station.
sentences.idiomatic: He made Li Guang spend the night under the station eaves.

sentences.chinese: 居無何，匈奴入遼西，殺太守，敗韓將軍。
sentences.literal: Before long had passed, the Xiongnu entered Liaoxi, killed the governor, and defeated General Han.
sentences.idiomatic: Not long afterward the Xiongnu struck Liaoxi, killed the governor there, and routed General Han Anguo.

sentences.chinese: 韓將軍後徙居右北平，死。
sentences.literal: General Han afterward was transferred to reside at Youbeiping and died.
sentences.idiomatic: General Han was later transferred to Youbeiping, where he died in office.

sentences.chinese: 於是上乃召拜廣為右北平太守。
sentences.literal: Thereupon the emperor summoned and appointed Guang governor of Youbeiping.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor then recalled Li Guang and named him governor of Youbeiping.

sentences.chinese: 廣請霸陵尉與俱，至軍而斬之，上書自陳謝罪。
sentences.literal: Guang requested the Baling post captain to go with him; when they reached the army he beheaded him, and submitted a memorial stating himself and apologizing for his crime.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang insisted that the Baling captain accompany him to headquarters and had him executed on arrival, then filed a memorial explaining himself and asking to be punished.

sentences.chinese: 上報曰：「將軍者，國之爪牙也。
sentences.literal: The emperor replied in a rescript, "The general is the claws and fangs of the state.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor answered: "A general is the fangs and claws of the realm.

sentences.chinese: 《司馬法》曰：『登車不式，遭喪不服，振旅撫師，以征不服，率三軍之心，同戰士之力，故怒形則千里竦，威振則萬物狀；
sentences.literal: The Sima fa says: 'Mount the chariot without saluting; meet mourning without donning mourning garments; rouse the army and comfort the hosts to punish those who do not submit; lead the hearts of the three armies and unite the strength of the fighting men—therefore when anger appears, a thousand li are awed; when might shakes forth, the myriad things take form;
sentences.idiomatic: The Military Canons of Sima say: 'Board your chariot without bowing in courtesy; on campaign set mourning aside; marshal the host and steady the ranks to chastise rebellion; bind the three armies to one purpose and the soldiery to one will—then a flash of wrath makes the realm tremble for a thousand li, and the sweep of your majesty gives shape to all beneath heaven;

sentences.chinese: 是以名聲暴於夷貉，威稜憺乎鄰國。』
sentences.literal: thereby fame is displayed among the Yi and Mo, and the edge of awe spreads to neighboring states.'"
sentences.idiomatic: so that your name thunders among the barbarians and the glint of your power overawes every neighbor.'"

sentences.chinese: 夫報忿除害，捐殘去殺，朕之所圖於將軍也；
sentences.literal: As for repaying resentment and removing harm, casting off cruelty and doing away with killing—that is what We intend for the general;
sentences.idiomatic: To settle scores with wrongdoers, root out evil, and curb needless bloodshed—that is what I expect of you as a commander;

sentences.chinese: 若乃免冠徒跣，稽顙請罪，豈朕之指哉！
sentences.literal: but as for taking off the cap, going barefoot, and knocking the forehead in begging forgiveness—is that Our intent!
sentences.idiomatic: not for you to bare your head, go barefoot, and beat your brow in the posture of a criminal—that was never my meaning!

sentences.chinese: 將軍其率師東轅，彌節白檀，以臨右北平盛秋。」
sentences.literal: General, lead your army with east-turning shafts, slow the pace at Baitan, and in the height of autumn come to Youbeiping."
sentences.idiomatic: Turn your column east, take up position at Baitan, and be in Youbeiping by the height of autumn."

sentences.chinese: 廣在郡，匈奴號曰「漢飛將軍」，避之，數歲不入界。
sentences.literal: While Guang was in the commandery, the Xiongnu called him "the Han Flying General," avoided him, and for several years did not enter the border.
sentences.idiomatic: While he held the commandery the Xiongnu dubbed him the Flying General of Han and gave his district a wide berth for years.

sentences.chinese: 廣出獵，見草中石，以為虎而射之，中石沒矢，視之，石也，他日射之，終不能入矣。
sentences.literal: Guang went out hunting; he saw a stone in the grass, thought it a tiger, and shot at it; he hit the stone and the arrow sank in; looking at it, it was a stone; another day he shot at it and in the end could not make it enter.
sentences.idiomatic: Once on a hunt he mistook a boulder in tall grass for a tiger and let fly; the shaft buried itself to the fletching in solid rock. When he saw what it was, he tried again on another day and could never repeat the shot.

sentences.chinese: 廣所居郡聞有虎，常自射之。
sentences.literal: In whatever commandery Guang dwelt, if tigers were heard of, he always shot them himself.
sentences.idiomatic: Wherever he was posted, if word came of a man-eater, he went after it himself.

sentences.chinese: 及居右北平射虎，虎騰傷廣，廣亦射殺之。
sentences.literal: When he dwelt at Youbeiping and shot a tiger, the tiger leaped up and wounded Guang; Guang also shot and killed it.
sentences.idiomatic: At Youbeiping a tiger he had wounded sprang and mauled him; he still brought it down with his bow.

sentences.chinese: 石建卒，上召廣代為郎中令。
sentences.literal: Shi Jian died; the emperor summoned Guang to replace him as superintendent of the palace gentlemen.
sentences.idiomatic: When Shi Jian died, the emperor called Li Guang to the capital to succeed him as superintendent of the palace gentlemen.

sentences.chinese: 元朔六年，廣復為將軍，從大將軍出定襄。
sentences.literal: In the sixth year of Yuanshuo Guang again became a general and followed the supreme commander out of Dingxiang.
sentences.idiomatic: In 123 BCE he was again given a general's commission and marched from Dingxiang under the supreme commander Wei Qing.

sentences.chinese: 諸將多中首虜率為侯者，而廣軍無功。
sentences.literal: Of the various generals many met the head-and-captive quota and became marquises, but Guang's army achieved no merit.
sentences.idiomatic: Most of the subordinate commanders earned enough kills to be ennobled; Li Guang's column alone came back empty-handed.

sentences.chinese: 後三歲，廣以郎中令將四千騎出右北平，博望侯張騫將萬騎與廣俱，異道。
sentences.literal: Three years later Guang, as superintendent of the palace gentlemen, led four thousand riders out of Youbeiping; the Marquis of Bowang Zhang Qian led ten thousand riders together with Guang, by a different route.
sentences.idiomatic: Three years later he led four thousand horse out of Youbeiping in his capacity as superintendent of gentlemen, while the Marquis of Bowang Zhang Qian took ten thousand cavalry by another line of march to support him.

sentences.chinese: 行數百里，匈奴左賢王將四萬騎圍廣，廣軍士皆恐，廣乃使其子敢往馳之。
sentences.literal: Having gone several hundred li, the Xiongnu Worthy King of the Left led forty thousand riders to surround Guang; Guang's officers and soldiers were all afraid, and Guang then sent his son Gan to gallop against them.
sentences.idiomatic: Several hundred li into the steppe the Worthy King of the Left ringed him with forty thousand horse. His men were terrified until Li Guang sent his son Li Gan to charge the encirclement.

sentences.chinese: 敢從數十騎直貫胡騎，出其左右而還，報廣曰：「胡虜易與耳。」
sentences.literal: Gan, with several dozen riders, went straight through the Hu riders, came out to their left and right and returned, reporting to Guang, "The Hu captives are easy to deal with."
sentences.idiomatic: Li Gan took a few dozen troopers, punched straight through the enemy line, wheeled around their flanks, and rode back to report, "The barbarians are nothing much."

sentences.chinese: 軍士乃安。
sentences.literal: The officers and soldiers then were reassured.
sentences.idiomatic: Only then did the ranks steady.

sentences.chinese: 為圜陳外鄉，胡急擊，矢下如雨。
sentences.literal: They made a circular formation facing outward; the Hu pressed the attack urgently; arrows fell like rain.
sentences.idiomatic: He formed a hollow square facing outward; the Xiongnu pressed the assault and arrows fell in sheets.

sentences.chinese: 漢兵死者過半，漢矢且盡。
sentences.literal: More than half the Han soldiers died; the Han arrows were nearly exhausted.
sentences.idiomatic: Casualties passed fifty percent and their quivers were almost empty.

sentences.chinese: 廣乃令持滿毋發，而廣身自以大黃射其裨將，殺數人，胡虜益解。
sentences.literal: Guang then ordered them to hold full draw and not release, while Guang himself with a great yellow crossbow shot their deputy generals, killing several men; the Hu captives gradually slackened.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang ordered his men to hold at full draw without loosing while he himself worked a heavy crossbow and dropped several of their subordinate commanders; the pressure on the ring began to ease.

sentences.chinese: 會暮，吏士無人色，而廣意氣自如，益治軍。
sentences.literal: It happened to be dusk; the clerks and soldiers had no human color, but Guang's spirit was as of itself; he all the more put the army in order.
sentences.idiomatic: Night came on; officers and men were grey-faced with exhaustion, but Li Guang was calm as ever and went about tightening the formation.

sentences.chinese: 軍中服其勇也。
sentences.literal: In the army they admired his bravery.
sentences.idiomatic: The whole command admired his nerve.

sentences.chinese: 明日，復力戰，而博望侯軍亦軍，匈奴乃解去。
sentences.literal: The next day they again fought with full force, and the Marquis of Bowang's army also arrived as an army; the Xiongnu then broke off and departed.
sentences.idiomatic: They fought again at daybreak, and when Zhang Qian's column finally came up the Xiongnu lifted the siege and withdrew.

sentences.chinese: 漢軍邑，弗能追。
sentences.literal: The Han army was low in spirit and could not pursue.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang's men were too shaken and worn to follow the retreating enemy.

sentences.chinese: 是時，廣軍幾沒，罷歸。
sentences.literal: At this time Guang's army was nearly annihilated; they were dismissed and returned.
sentences.idiomatic: His force had been all but wiped out; they were stood down and marched home.

sentences.chinese: 漢法，博望侯後期，當死，贖為庶人。
sentences.literal: By Han law the Marquis of Bowang was late to the rendezvous and should have died; he ransomed himself and became a commoner.
sentences.idiomatic: Under military law Zhang Qian's late arrival was a capital offense; he bought off the sentence and was reduced to commoner status.

sentences.chinese: 廣軍自當，亡賞。
sentences.literal: Guang's army had borne the brunt itself; there was no reward.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang had held the enemy alone and received no enfeoffment or bounty.

sentences.chinese: 初，廣與從弟李蔡俱為郎，事文帝。
sentences.literal: In the beginning Guang together with his cousin Li Cai were both gentlemen-attendants, serving Emperor Wen.
sentences.idiomatic: In his youth Li Guang and his cousin Li Cai had both served as gentlemen-attendants under Emperor Wen.

sentences.chinese: 景帝時，蔡積功至二千石。
sentences.literal: In Emperor Jing's time Cai accumulated merit up to two-thousand-dan rank.
sentences.idiomatic: Under Emperor Jing, Li Cai rose through successive posts until he held a two-thousand-dan salary.

sentences.chinese: 武帝元朔中，為輕車將軍，從大將軍擊右賢王，有功中率，封為樂安侯。
sentences.literal: In the Yuanshuo period of Emperor Wu he was light-chariot general; following the supreme commander he struck the Worthy King of the Right, had merit meeting the quota, and was enfeoffed as Marquis of Le'an.
sentences.idiomatic: In Emperor Wu's Yuanshuo years he led the light chariots under the supreme commander against the Worthy King of the Right, cleared the kill quota for enfeoffment, and was made Marquis of Le'an.

sentences.chinese: 元狩二年，代公孫弘為丞相。
sentences.literal: In the second year of Yuanshou he replaced Gongsun Hong as chancellor.
sentences.idiomatic: In 121 BCE he succeeded Gongsun Hong as chancellor of the empire.

sentences.chinese: 蔡為人在下中，名聲出廣下遠甚，然廣不得爵邑，官不過九卿。
sentences.literal: Cai's person was in the lower middle grade; his fame fell far below Guang's, yet Guang did not obtain rank or fief; his office did not exceed the nine ministers.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Cai was a mediocre man whose reputation could not compare with Li Guang's, yet it was Cai who won a marquisate while Li Guang never rose above the nine ministers and took no fief.

sentences.chinese: 廣之軍吏及士卒或取封侯。
sentences.literal: Among Guang's army clerks and soldiers some obtained enfeoffment as marquises.
sentences.idiomatic: Some of Li Guang's own subalterns and privates had been ennobled for merit.

sentences.chinese: 廣與望氣王朔語云：「自漢擊匈奴，廣未嘗不在其中，而諸妄校尉已下，材能不及中，以軍功取侯者數十人。
sentences.literal: Guang spoke with Wang Shuo who read omens in the clouds, saying that since the Han struck the Xiongnu, Guang had never not been among them, yet of the various colonels and below, those whose talents and ability did not reach the middle grade—several tens of men had taken marquisates by military merit.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang said to Wang Shuo, who read omens in the clouds, that he had fought in every Han campaign against the Xiongnu while dozens of colonels and men beneath them—talent no better than middling—had been ennobled for their service alone.

sentences.chinese: 廣不為後人，然終無尺寸功以得封邑者，何也？
sentences.literal: Guang is not behind others, yet in the end there is no merit even the size of a foot or an inch by which to obtain a fief—why is that?
sentences.idiomatic: I have never lagged behind my peers, so why have I never earned even the smallest fief?

sentences.chinese: 豈吾相不當侯邪？」
sentences.literal: Could it be that my physiognomy is not suited to being a marquis?"
sentences.idiomatic: Could it be I was never fated by my face to hold a marquisate?"

sentences.chinese: 朔曰：「將軍自念，豈嘗有恨者乎？」
sentences.literal: Shuo said, "General, reflect yourself—have you ever had something you regret?"
sentences.idiomatic: Wang Shuo asked, "Think back, General—is there anything you still regret?"

sentences.chinese: 廣曰：「吾為隴西守，羌嘗反，吾誘降者八百餘人，詐而同日殺之，至今恨獨此耳。」
sentences.literal: Guang said, "When I was governor of Longxi, the Qiang once rebelled; I lured more than eight hundred who surrendered, deceived them, and killed them all the same day—to this day my regret is only this."
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang answered, "When I governed Longxi the Qiang rose; I persuaded more than eight hundred of them to lay down their arms, then broke faith and had every one executed the same day. That is the one act I cannot forget."

sentences.chinese: 朔曰：「禍莫大於殺已降，此乃將軍所以不得侯者也。」
sentences.literal: Shuo said, "No misfortune is greater than killing those who have already surrendered; this is why the general has not obtained a marquisate."
sentences.idiomatic: Wang Shuo replied, "Nothing brings down a heavier curse than slaughtering men who have already yielded. That, General, is why the marquisate has never come."

sentences.chinese: 廣歷七郡太守，前後四十餘年，得賞賜，輒分其戲下，飲食與士卒共之。
sentences.literal: Guang passed through seven commanderies as governor; for more than forty years in all, whenever he obtained rewards or gifts he always divided them among those beneath his banner; drink and food he shared with officers and soldiers.
sentences.idiomatic: He governed seven frontier commanderies over more than forty years; every imperial gift went to his men, and he ate and drank with the ranks as they did.

sentences.chinese: 家無余財，終不言生產事。
sentences.literal: His household had no surplus wealth; to the end he did not speak of matters of production.
sentences.idiomatic: He died without savings and never bothered with land or trade.

sentences.chinese: 為人長，爰臂，其善射亦天性，雖子孫他人學者莫能及。
sentences.literal: In person he was tall, with long arms; his skill at archery was also inborn nature—though sons, grandsons, and others studied, none could match him.
sentences.idiomatic: He was a tall man with long apelike arms, and his archery was a gift of nature that neither his heirs nor any pupil could duplicate.

sentences.chinese: 廣吶口少言，與人居，則畫地為軍陳，射闊狹以飲。
sentences.literal: Guang was slow of mouth and spoke little; when with others he would draw on the ground army formations and shoot at narrow and wide to determine drinking.
sentences.idiomatic: He was a man of few words; in company he would sketch battle lines in the dust and wager drinks on contests of archery at narrow targets.

sentences.chinese: 專以射為戲。
sentences.literal: He devoted himself to taking archery as amusement.
sentences.idiomatic: Archery was his only pastime.

sentences.chinese: 將兵，乏絕處見水，士卒不盡飲，不近水；
sentences.literal: When leading troops, in places of want and exhaustion when water was seen, if the soldiers had not all drunk, he did not approach the water;
sentences.idiomatic: On campaign, if his men had not all drunk from a found water source, he would not touch it himself;

sentences.chinese: 不盡餐，不嚐食；
sentences.literal: if they had not all finished eating, he did not taste food;
sentences.idiomatic: if they had not all been fed, he would not eat;

sentences.chinese: 寬緩不苛，士以此愛樂為用。
sentences.literal: he was lenient and easy, not harsh; by this the officers loved and delighted to be employed by him.
sentences.idiomatic: yet his discipline was loose rather than cruel, and for that the troops loved him and fought willingly for him.

sentences.chinese: 其射，見敵，非在數十步之內，度不中不發，發即應弦而倒。
sentences.literal: As for his shooting, when he saw the enemy, if they were not within several tens of paces, he gauged that he would not hit and did not release; when he released, then they responded to the string and fell.
sentences.idiomatic: He would not loose an arrow until the target stood within a few dozen paces and he was sure of the shot; when he did shoot, the man dropped with the twang of the bowstring.

sentences.chinese: 用此，其將數困辱，及射猛獸，亦數為所傷雲。
sentences.literal: By this means, as a general he several times met distress and humiliation; and in shooting fierce beasts, he also several times was wounded by them, it is said.
sentences.idiomatic: That habit of closing to point-blank range cost him more than one battlefield setback, and the same boldness brought him repeated wounds from the beasts he hunted.

sentences.chinese: 元狩四年，大將軍票騎將軍大擊匈奴，廣數自請行。
sentences.literal: In the fourth year of Yuanshou the supreme commander and the swift-crossing general greatly struck the Xiongnu; Guang repeatedly requested permission to go.
sentences.idiomatic: In 119 BCE the court launched the great double offensive under Wei Qing and Huo Qubing; Li Guang petitioned again and again to take the field.

sentences.chinese: 上以為老，不許；
sentences.literal: The emperor thought him old and did not grant permission;
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor judged him too old and refused;

sentences.chinese: 良久乃許之，以為前將軍。
sentences.literal: after a long while he then granted it, making him vanguard general.
sentences.idiomatic: after long insistence he relented and named him vanguard general.

sentences.chinese: 大將軍青出塞，捕虜知單于所居，乃自以精兵走之，而令廣並於右將軍軍，出東道。
sentences.literal: Supreme Commander Qing went beyond the barrier; capturing prisoners he learned where the Chanyu dwelt; then he himself with picked troops hastened thither, and ordered Guang to merge with the army of the general of the right, going out by the eastern road.
sentences.idiomatic: Wei Qing crossed the frontier, learned from prisoners where the Chanyu lay, and drove for that point with his elite while ordering Li Guang to combine his column with the general of the right's and take the longer eastern route.

sentences.chinese: 東道少回遠，大軍行，水草少，其勢不屯行。
sentences.literal: The eastern road was somewhat circuitous and far; for a great army to march, grass and water were few; in its situation it could not halt and march.
sentences.idiomatic: The eastern track swung wide, offered little forage or water, and could not support a large force moving in stages.
